did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning-based Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models (VGG16, VGG19, and Inception V3) for feature extraction and machine learning classifiers (SVM, KNN, RF, NN, LR, Ada) for the classification of dermoscopy images of skin lesions as benign or malignant.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the collection and pre-processing of a dataset of 3297 skin cancer images, the application of deep learning models (VGG16, VGG19, Inception V3) for feature extraction, and the use of machine learning classifiers (SVM, KNN, RF, NN, LR, AdaBoost) for classification. It reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 83.2% accuracy) and concludes with results from their own implementation, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Disease Classification using Dermoscopy Images through Deep Feature Learning Models and Machine Learning Classifiers
journal: IEEE EUROCON 2021 - 19th International Conference on Smart Technologies
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of benign and malignant skin lesions using dermoscopy images to assist in early melanoma detection. The authors utilized a publicly available dataset of 3,297 images, which were pre-processed to remove hair artifacts and resized to a uniform format. Deep learning models VGG16, VGG19, and Inception V3 were employed for feature extraction, followed by classification using machine learning algorithms including SVM, KNN, Random Forest, Neural Network, Logistic Regression, and AdaBoost. The performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC metrics. The results demonstrated that the combination of the Inception V3 model with a Neural Network classifier achieved the highest accuracy of 83.2%, outperforming other tested configurations.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task. The dataset comprised 3297 images total, with the benign class representing one half of the 'two different types of moles' used for training and testing.

One of the two classes in the binary classification task. The paper explicitly states the dataset consists of 'two different types of moles' (benign and malignant) and focuses on classifying them as such. Melanoma is mentioned as the most deadly form of skin cancer and the primary context for early detection, but the experimental labels are binary (benign vs. malignant).

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions as benign or malignant
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is the 'classification of moles as benign or malignant' using dermoscopy images. The dataset consists of two classes: benign tumor and malignant tumor. The models (VGG16, VGG19, Inception V3) extract features which are then passed to machine learning classifiers (SVM, KNN, RF, NN, LR, AdaBoost) to predict the discrete diagnostic category. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Cancer Malignant vs Benign Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of dermoscopy images of skin lesions, sourced from Kaggle. The dataset contains images of two different types of moles (benign and malignant).

use.training: 2637 images were used for training purposes after pre-processing (resizing, cropping, and hair removal using the Dull Razor method).
use.testing: 660 images were used for testing purposes.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the performance of different machine learning classifiers (KNN, SVM, RF, NN, LR, AdaBoost) combined with CNN models (VGG16, VGG19, Inception V3) by plotting sensitivity against specificity on several thresholds.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC, and compared qualitatively against other methods.

Kaggle Skin Cancer Malignant vs Benign D.notes: The paper reports that the Inception V3 model combined with the Neural Network classifier achieved the maximum accuracy of 83.2%. ROC-AUC curves were used to evaluate performance by plotting sensitivity against specificity.

architecture_used.main_model: VGG16, VGG19, and Inception V3 (used for feature extraction) combined with Machine Learning classifiers (SVM, KNN, RF, NN, LR, AdaBoost) for classification.
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; standard ImageNet pretraining is implied for VGG and Inception models but not stated.
architecture_used.input_size: 224 x 244 pixels (original resolution), resized to a uniform format for processing.

architecture_details.feature_extractor: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). Specifically: VGG16, VGG19, and Inception V3. Inception V3 is described as comprising 48 layers deep, using multiple convolutional layers (filter sizes 7x7, 5x5, 3x3, 1x1) and pooling layers (max pooling, average pooling, min pooling) with strides of 1 and 2.
architecture_details.classifier: Machine Learning classifiers: Support Vector Machine (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Random Forest (RF), Neural Network (NN), Logistic Regression (LR), and AdaBoost.
architecture_details.overall_pipeline: Input images -> Pre-processing (Dull Razor for hair removal, resizing, cropping) -> CNN Feature Extraction -> ML Classification.

training_details.dataset_split: 2637 images for training, 660 images for testing.
training_details.preprocessing: Hair removal using Dull Razor method (greyscale morphological operation, bi-linear interpolation), resizing, and cropping.

classification_or_output_head.type: Machine Learning Classifiers (SVM, KNN, RF, NN, LR, AdaBoost).
classification_or_output_head.metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 Score, AUC.

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to convert non-uniform images to a single format
Image cropping to convert images to a uniform format

Hair removal using the Dull Razor method, which involves: 1) Greyscale morphological operation to detect hair position, 2) Verification of hair structure and shape, 3) Replacement of hair pixels using bi-linear interpolation, 4) Application of an adaptive median filter to smooth replaced hair pixels
conclusions: The study concludes that combining deep learning-based feature extraction with machine learning classifiers is an efficient and cost-effective approach for classifying dermoscopy images of skin lesions as benign or malignant.

The Inception V3 model combined with a Neural Network (NN) classifier achieved the highest performance, with an accuracy of 83.2%, outperforming other tested configurations (VGG16 and VGG19 with various classifiers).

The proposed method significantly reduces the time and effort required for dermatologists to distinguish between benign and malignant tumors compared to manual detection, which is described as tedious and time-consuming.

The authors acknowledge that the current results can be improved in future work by increasing the number of images in the dataset and by testing other CNN models to enhance accuracy and other performance parameters.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, publicly available Kaggle dataset (3,297 images) with a limited training set (2,637 images), which may restrict generalizability and model robustness.

The dataset contains only two classes (benign vs. malignant), excluding other common skin cancer types like Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) mentioned in the introduction, limiting clinical scope.

No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the same Kaggle dataset used for training, raising concerns about overfitting and data leakage risks inherent to single-source datasets.

The reported accuracy of 83.2% is modest compared to dermatologist-level performance (e.g., Esteva et al. reported higher accuracy in prior work), suggesting the method may not yet be clinically competitive for high-stakes diagnosis.
The paper does not report standard deviations, confidence intervals, or statistical significance tests for the performance metrics, making it difficult to assess the reliability of the results.

Preprocessing steps (hair removal via Dull Razor, resizing, cropping) were applied, but no data augmentation techniques were used, which is a limitation for deep learning models prone to overfitting on small datasets.

The study uses a hybrid approach (CNN feature extraction + ML classifiers) rather than end-to-end deep learning, which may limit the ability of the model to learn optimal feature representations for classification.
The authors acknowledge that future work is needed to increase dataset size and test other CNN models, indicating current results are preliminary and not definitive.
